title: Dog Ate Dark Chocolate: Emergency Triage & Treatment Guide
meta_desc: Dark chocolate is highly toxic to dogs. Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and what steps you need to take immediately to save your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Dark chocolate ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that requires immediate veterinary intervention. Save the packaging and contact a vet right away, as the high concentration of stimulants can cause rapid internal damage.

What causes dark chocolate toxicity in dogs?

Dark chocolate contains high levels of theobromine and caffeine, two stimulants known as methylxanthines. While humans process these easily, dogs metabolize them very slowly. This leads to a dangerous buildup in their system that can affect the heart, central nervous system, and kidneys.

Is dark chocolate ingestion a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Because dark chocolate has a much higher concentration of cocoa than milk chocolate, even small amounts can be fatal.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison helpline immediately.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as internal damage can occur quickly.

Why should I take a photo of the chocolate packaging for the vet?

  • Take a clear photo of the wrapper, specifically the ingredients and the cocoa percentage (e.g., 70% Cacao).
  • This allows the veterinary team to calculate the exact dose of theobromine relative to your dog's weight.
  • The calculation determines whether the situation requires immediate decontamination or intensive monitoring.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of chocolate toxicosis in dogs, stabilization of the symptomatic animal is a priority. Clinical signs usually occur within 6-12 hours of ingestion and may initially include polydipsia, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal distention, and restlessness, progressing to hyperactivity, polyuria, ataxia, rigidity, tremors, and seizures. Tachycardia, premature ventricular contractions, tachypnea, cyanosis, hypertension, hyperthermia, bradycardia, hypotension, or coma may also occur; death is generally due to cardiac arrhythmias, hyperthermia, or respiratory failure. Methocarbamol (50-220 mg/kg, slow IV, not exceeding 330 mg/kg/day) or diazepam (0.5-2 mg/kg, slow IV) can be administered for tremors and mild seizures, while barbiturates may be required for severe seizures.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2967)

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of chocolate poisoning in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include extreme thirst, vomiting, diarrhea, restlessness, a racing heart rate, tremors, and in severe cases, seizures.

faqs.question: How much dark chocolate is too much?
faqs.answer: For many dogs, as little as a fraction of an ounce of dark chocolate per pound of body weight can cause severe toxicity.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Only induce vomiting if specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian, as it can be dangerous depending on your dog's condition.
